title: Amazon Associates in 2026: Exact Requirements and How to Pass Review
excerpt: Amazon closes accounts that don't hit 3 sales in 180 days. Here's every requirement — and the strategy to meet them before they check.
imageAlt: Amazon Associates dashboard showing earnings and product links
seoDescription: Amazon Associates requirements for 2026 — content, traffic, sales quotas, and disclosure rules you must know before applying.
body:
Amazon Associates is the most widely known affiliate program in the world, and also one of the most commonly misunderstood. Getting in is relatively easy. Staying in is where most affiliates fail — because Amazon will close your account if you don't make three qualifying sales within 180 days of approval. Here's everything you actually need to know.

## What Amazon Checks During Review

Amazon reviewers look for three things: original content, a legitimate reason someone would visit your site, and recent activity. "Recent" means posts published within the last 60 days. A site with 20 articles written 18 months ago and nothing since reads as abandoned.

Your site must have a custom domain (not a free Wix or Blogger subdomain), a visible privacy policy, and content that adds genuine value. Amazon's terms are explicit: original content is defined as commentary, analysis, or transformation that creates additional value — not scraped product descriptions.

## The 3-Sale Requirement Is the Real Test

Approval is automatic for most sites that meet the basic criteria. The harder challenge is generating three qualifying purchases within your first 180 days. This is where new affiliates get caught out — they get approved, celebrate, and don't aggressively drive traffic to their affiliate links.

Three purchases is a low bar if you have any audience at all. Plan your first affiliate posts specifically around high-buying-intent keywords: product reviews, comparison guides, "best X for Y" articles. These convert at 3–8% versus 0.5–1% for informational content.

> If you don't hit three sales in six months, your account closes automatically and you must reapply from scratch.

## Content Volume and Quality

Amazon doesn't publish a minimum article count, but 10 to 15 well-written posts before applying is the consistently reported sweet spot in affiliate communities. Your posts should include actual product recommendations relevant to your niche, not just general information.

Every product page you link to should be available in the Amazon marketplace for your regional Associates account. Linking to out-of-stock or region-mismatched products wastes clicks and doesn't count toward your qualifying sales.

## Traffic Requirements

Amazon does not specify a minimum traffic threshold. Sites with 500 monthly visitors get approved. The relevant metric is whether your traffic is real and targeted — not inflated by bots or purchased clicks. During the 180-day probation period, drive every ounce of organic, social, and email traffic you have to your affiliate content.

## Disclosure Requirements

This is non-negotiable: you must display the Amazon Associates disclosure on every page that contains affiliate links. The exact required language is: "As an Amazon Associate I earn from qualifying purchases." It must be clearly visible — buried in a footer in 8px gray text will get your account flagged.

Add the disclosure above the fold on review posts and directly before any affiliate link cluster in listicles. No exceptions.

## After the First 180 Days

Once you've made three sales, your account enters good standing. From there, you earn commissions on every qualifying purchase — not just the linked product. If someone clicks your link for a kitchen knife and buys a blender, a television, and three books in the same session, you earn commission on all of it within a 24-hour window.

The category commission rates vary widely: luxury beauty at 10%, physical books at 4.5%, electronics at 3%, and Amazon Fresh at 1%. Build your content strategy around higher-margin categories where possible.
